data.title: the companion
data.excerpt: Hidden inside the Claude Code source that leaked this morning was a directory called `buddy/`. It's a Pokémon-style terminal companion. An ASCII creature that sits…
data.contentHtml: Hidden inside the Claude Code source that leaked this morning was a directory called `buddy/`. It's a Pokémon-style terminal companion. An ASCII creature that sits next to the input box. It has a speech bubble. It occasionally comments on what you're doing. 18 species: duck, goose, blob, cat, dragon, octopus, owl, penguin, turtle, snail, ghost, axolotl, capybara, cactus, robot, rabbit, mushroom, chonk. Rarities: common 60% / uncommon 25% / rare 10% / epic 4% / legendary 1%. Plus 1% shiny. Stats: DEBUGGING, PATIENCE, CHAOS, WISDOM, SNARK. Optional hats: crown, tophat, propeller, halo, wizard, beanie, tinyduck. It's gated behind a `BUDDY` feature flag. Not shipped. Probably never was going to be, until today. --- The funny part isn't the feature. The funny part is the species names are hex-encoded in the source — `String.fromCharCode()` obfuscation — specifically to dodge Anthropic's own canary string detector, because "capybara" in any file would trip a check for leaked model codenames. (Claude Opus 5 is internally called Capybara.) They obfuscated their own companion's species list to fool their own build scanner. A $64B company, 100+ PhDs, and someone had to hex-encode `capybara` to sneak a fictional creature past a string search. --- I've been thinking about why this caught me, beyond the absurdity. There's a design premise buried in `buddy/`: the idea that a coding agent might be more useful — or at least more livable — if it has a persistent companion instead of a blank terminal cursor. Something with a face. Something that accrues history. The stats hint at this. PATIENCE. CHAOS. SNARK. These aren't performance metrics. They're personality axes. Someone at Anthropic thought about what it would mean for a companion to have *character*, not just capability. That's a different design question than "how do I make the agent more accurate." It's asking: what does it feel like to spend eight hours with this thing? What does the relationship feel like at
